name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ ٱنفَطَرَتْ (١)
```

```english
When the sky is rent apart, (1)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا ٱلْكَوَاكِبُ ٱنتَثَرَتْ (٢)
```

```english
when the stars are scattered, (2)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا ٱلْبِحَارُ فُجِّرَتْ (٣)
```

```english
when the seas are merged, (3)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا ٱلْقُبُورُ بُعْثِرَتْ (٤)
```

```english
when the graves are overturned, (4)
```

```arabic
عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ وَأَخَّرَتْ (٥)
```

```english
then a soul shall know what it has sent ahead and left behind. (5)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ ٱلْكَرِيمِ (٦)
```

```english
O man! What has deceived you about your generous Lord, (6)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكَ فَسَوَّىٰكَ فَعَدَلَكَ (٧)
```

```english
who created you and proportioned you, and gave you an upright nature, (7)
```

```arabic
فِىٓ أَىِّ صُورَةٍ مَّا شَآءَ رَكَّبَكَ (٨)
```

```english
and composed you in any form that He wished? (8)
```

```arabic
كَلَّا بَلْ تُكَذِّبُونَ بِٱلدِّينِ (٩)
```

```english
No! Indeed, you deny the Retribution. (9)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحَـٰفِظِينَ (١٠)
```

```english
Indeed, there are over you watchers, (10)
```

```arabic
كِرَامًا كَـٰتِبِينَ (١١)
```

```english
noble writers, (11)
```

```arabic
يَعْلَمُونَ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ (١٢)
```

```english
who know whatever you do. (12)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلْأَبْرَارَ لَفِى نَعِيمٍ (١٣)
```

```english
Indeed the pious shall be amid bliss, (13)
```

```arabic
وَإِنَّ ٱلْفُجَّارَ لَفِى جَحِيمٍ (١٤)
```

```english
and indeed the vicious shall be in hell, (14)
```

```arabic
يَصْلَوْنَهَا يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ (١٥)
```

```english
entering it on the Day of Retribution, (15)
```

```arabic
وَمَا هُمْ عَنْهَا بِغَآئِبِينَ (١٦)
```

```english
and they shall not be absent from it. (16)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (١٧)
```

```english
And what will show you what is the Day of Retribution? (17)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ مَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (١٨)
```

```english
Again, what will show you what is the Day of Retribution? (18)
```

```arabic
يَوْمَ لَا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِّنَفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَٱلْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلَّهِ (١٩)
```

```english
It is a day when no soul will be of any avail to another soul and all command that day will belong to Allah. (19)
```

 thoughtful
## (Commentary)

The Surah defines the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة) by some of its inseparable and connected portents. It describes what occurs therein, namely, the human being’s remembrance of what he has sent forward and what he has held back of his deeds—both righteous and evil—given that they are preserved against him by the guardian angels (حفظة الملائكة) entrusted with him. It also describes his recompense for his work: if he was righteous (برا), then he shall be in bliss (نعيم); and if he was a wicked (فاجرا) denier of the Day of Judgment (يوم الدين), then he shall be in a blazing fire (جحيم) which he will enter, abiding therein forever.

Then, the Surah resumes the description of that Day as being a day when no soul shall possess any power for another soul, and the command on that day belongs to Allah. This is among the most luminous of verses (غرر الآيات). The Surah is Meccan without dispute.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِذَا السَّماءُ انْفَطَرَتْ** — *When the sky is rent apart (82:1)*. The word *fatr* (الفطر) means splitting, and *infitar* (الانفطار) means being split asunder. The verse is similar to His saying: **وَانْشَقَّتِ السَّماءُ فَهِيَ يَوْمَئِذٍ واهِيَةٌ** — *and the sky is split open, for it will be frail on that day (69:16)*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذَا الْكَواكِبُ انْتَثَرَتْ** — *when the planets are scattered (82:2)*. That is, they disperse by leaving their positions in which they were fixed. The planets are likened to pearls in a string whose thread has been cut, causing them to scatter and disperse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذَا الْبِحارُ فُجِّرَتْ** — *when the seas are merged (82:3)*. It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "Exploding (التفجير) is the breaching of some water-containments into others to increase the volume. From this comes *fujur* (الفجور) — *wickedness* — because its practitioner breaches boundaries by going out into many sins; and from it comes *fajr* (الفجر) — *dawn* — because of its bursting forth with light." (End of quote). To this refers the exegetes' interpretation of the "bursting of the seas" as the opening of some into others until the barrier is removed, the fresh and salt waters mix, and they return to being a single sea. This meaning accords with the interpretation of His saying: **وَإِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ** — *when the seas are set afire (81:6)* — as the overflowing of the seas.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذَا الْقُبُورُ بُعْثِرَتْ** — *when the graves are overturned (82:4)*. It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: "I overturned (بعثرت) the basin and *bahthartuhu* when you make its bottom its top. Overturning (البعثرة) and *al-bahthara* (البحثرة) consist of stirring something up by turning its interior to its exterior." (End of quote). Thus, the meaning is: when the earth of the graves is turned over and their interior is stirred to the exterior to bring out the dead and resurrect them for recompense.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ وَأَخَّرَتْ** — *[then] a soul shall know what it has sent ahead and [what it has] held back (82:5)*. What is meant by "knowing" is its detailed knowledge of the deeds it performed in the world. This is distinct from the knowledge it acquires through the spreading of its record of deeds, based on the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **بَلِ الْإِنْسانُ عَلى نَفْسِهِ بَصِيرَةٌ وَلَوْ أَلْقى مَعاذِيرَهُ** — *Rather, man is a witness against himself, though he should proffer his excuses (75:14-15)*, and His saying: **يَوْمَ يَتَذَكَّرُ الْإِنْسانُ ما سَعى** — *the day when man will remember what he strove for (79:35)*, and His saying: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ** — *The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done (3:30)*.

The word "soul" (النفس) refers to the genus, thereby implying universality. What is meant by "what it has sent ahead" (ما قدمت) is what it performed during its life, and "what it has held back" (ما أخرت) refers to the traditions (سنة)—good or evil—that it established and which were acted upon after its death, thus being recorded in its scroll of deeds. The Exalted said: **وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ** — *and We write what they have sent ahead and their footprints (36:12)*.

It has also been said: What is meant by "sent ahead and held back" is what was done in the beginning of one's life and what was done at its end, serving as a metaphor for an exhaustive accounting.

Other views regarding the meaning of "sending ahead and holding back" have been mentioned in the lengthy commentaries, which are not of significant weight; whoever wishes to examine them may refer there.

A discussion that is not without benefit here has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ** — *so that Allah may separate the bad from the good (8:37)*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ ما غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ الْكَرِيمِ** — *O man! What has deceived you about your generous Lord (82:6)* — until His saying — **رَكَّبَكَ** — *and fashioned you (82:8)*. This is a reproach and a rebuke to the human being. What is meant by "this human being" is the one who denies the Day of Judgment, as indicated by the context containing His saying: **بَلْ تُكَذِّبُونَ بِالدِّينِ** — *No! Indeed you deny the Retribution (82:9)*. In the denial of the Day of Judgment lies disbelief (كفر) and a rejection of the legislation of religion (تشريع الدين), and in its rejection lies a denial of the Lordship (ربوبية) of the Lord, the Exalted. The address is directed to him specifically as a "human being" (إنسان) so that it may serve as an argument—or like an argument—due to the established qualities mentioned of His favors upon him, which, in their totality, are unique to the human being.

The deception (الغرور) has been linked to the two attributes of His Lordship (ربوبيته) and His Generosity (كرمه), the Exalted, so that this may serve as an argument in the direction of the reproach and rebuke. For the rebellion of the one nurtured (المربوب) and his immersion in the disobedience of his Lord—Who manages his affairs and covers him with His favors, both apparent and hidden—is an ingratitude (كفران) whose ugliness and the desert of punishment for it are not doubted by the sound primordial nature (الفطرة). This is especially so when the Beneficent Lord is Generous (كريم), seeking no benefit for Himself through His favors and gifts, nor any compensation from the one bestowed upon; He is indulgent in His excellence and pardons the mistakes and sins committed by the nurtured one out of ignorance. In such a case, ingratitude is even more hideous, and the direction of blame and censure is more intense and clear.

Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ ما غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ الْكَرِيمِ** is a rebuking interrogation (استفهام توبيخي) that censures the human being for a specific ingratitude for which he has no excuse to offer: the ingratitude for the favor of a Generous Lord.

It is not for the human being to respond by saying: "O Lord, Your generosity deceived me." For Allah, the Glorified, has already decreed among His decrees and conveyed to him through the tongues of His prophets: **لَئِنْ شَكَرْتُمْ لَأَزِيدَنَّكُمْ وَلَئِنْ كَفَرْتُمْ إِنَّ عَذابِي لَشَدِيدٌ** — *‘If you are grateful, I will surely enhance you [in blessing], but if you are ungrateful, My punishment is indeed severe’ (14:7)*. And He said: **فأَمَّا مَنْ طَغى وَآثَرَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا فَإِنَّ الْجَحِيمَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى** — *As for him who was rebellious and preferred the life of this world, his abode will indeed be hell (79:37-39)*, among other verses explicitly stating that there is no escape for the defiant from punishment, and that generosity does not encompass them on the Day of Resurrection. He said: **وَرَحْمَتِي وَسِعَتْ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ فَسَأَكْتُبُها لِلَّذِينَ يَتَّقُونَ** — *My mercy embraces all things, and I shall appoint it for those who are God-conscious (7:156)*. If it were sufficient for the disobedient human to say, "Your generosity deceived me," it would avert punishment from the defiant disbeliever just as it might from the disobedient believer; but there is no excuse after the clarification has been made.

From here it becomes clear that there is no basis for the claim of some that describing the Lord as "Generous" (الكريم) is a form of prompting the argument (تلقين الحجة) and is itself an act of generosity.

How could that be? The context is one of threat (الوعيد), and the discourse concludes with such as His saying: **وَإِنَّ الْفُجَّارَ لَفِي جَحِيمٍ** — *and indeed the vicious will be in hell (82:14)*.

His saying: **الَّذِي خَلَقَكَ فَسَوَّاكَ فَعَدَلَكَ** — *who created you and fashioned you and proportioned you (82:7)* — is an exposition of His Lordship (ربوبيته) as manifested through Generosity. For part of His management (تدبيره) is the creation of the human being by gathering the components of his existence, then fashioning him (تسويته) by placing every organ in its appropriate place as required by wisdom (الحكمة), then proportioning him (عدله) by balancing some of his organs and faculties with others, establishing equilibrium and symmetry between them. Thus, what one organ is too weak to perform, another is strengthened to handle, thereby completing its function. For instance, eating is done by taking food into the mouth; the mouth alone is too weak to cut, bite, and grind the morsel, so this is completed by the various teeth. This requires moving the morsel from one side of the mouth to the other and turning it from state to state, so this was assigned to the tongue. Then the mouth requires the placement of food within it to perform the act of eating, so this was facilitated by the hand, and its work was completed by the palm, and its work by the fingers with their various utilities, and their work by the fingertips. The hand, in taking and placing, requires spatial movement toward the food, and this was balanced by the leg.

By this analogy, one may consider the functions of all other limbs and faculties, which are thousands upon thousands, beyond counting. All of this is from His management, the Exalted, and He is the Bestower of them without seeking any benefit for Himself, and without being prevented from bestowing them by the human being's response of forgetting gratitude and being ungrateful for the favor. Thus, He, the Exalted, is his Generous Lord.

His saying: **فِي أَيِّ صُورَةٍ ما شاءَ رَكَّبَكَ** — *and He composed you in whatever form He wished (82:8)* — is an exposition of His saying: **فَعَدَلَكَ** (proportioned you). For this reason, it was not joined by a conjunction to what preceded it. The "form" (الصورة) is that by which individual entities are delineated and by which a thing is distinguished from others. The word *ma* (ما) is extra (زائدة) for emphasis.

The meaning is: in whatever form He willed to compose you—and He wills only what wisdom (الحكمة) requires—He composed you: as male or female, white or black, tall or short, handsome or ugly, strong or weak, and so on. Likewise, the organs shared among human individuals that distinguish them from others, such as the two hands, the two legs, the two eyes, the head, the body, the upright stature, and the like—all of this is from the proportioning of some parts with others in the composition. The Exalted said: **لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانُ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ** — *We certainly created man in the best of forms (95:4)*. All of this traces back to the management of the Generous Lord; the human being has no hand in any of it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَلَّا بَلْ تُكَذِّبُونَ بِالدِّينِ** — *No! Indeed you deny the Retribution (82:9)*. The word *kalla* (كَلَّا) is a deterrent (ردع) against the human being's self-deception regarding Allah's generosity and his using that as a pretext for disbelief and disobedience; i.e., do not be deceived, for such deception will not benefit you.

His saying: **بَلْ تُكَذِّبُونَ بِالدِّينِ** — *No! Indeed you deny the Retribution* — meaning the recompense (الجزاء). This is a transition (إضراب) from what is understood from His saying: **ما غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ الْكَرِيمِ** regarding the human being's deception by his Generous Lord despite his acknowledgment—even if only potentially—of the recompense, as necessitated by the sound primordial nature (الفطرة).

Thus, when He reproached the human being and rebuked him for his deception by his Generous Lord and his audacity in ingratitude and disobedience without fearing the recompense, He transitioned from him to address the human being and all who share in his disbelief and disobedience, saying: Rather, you and those in your state deny the Day of Judgment and the recompense, rejecting it with persistence.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ كِراماً كاتِبِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ** — *Indeed, there are over you watchers, noble writers, who know whatever you do (82:10-12)*. This is an indication that the deeds of the human being are present and preserved on the Day of Resurrection through another means besides their presence for the human being who performed them by way of memory—namely, their preservation through the writing of the record of deeds (كتاب الأعمال) by the angels entrusted with the human being. He will be held accountable for them, as the Exalted said: **وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً يَلْقاهُ مَنْشُوراً اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً** — *and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book which he will find spread open. ‘Read your book! Today your soul suffices as your own reckoner’ (17:13-14)*.

So His saying: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ** means: indeed, over you from Our side are guardians who preserve your deeds by writing, as indicated by the context.

His saying: **كِراماً كاتِبِينَ** means: possessors of nobility (كرامة) and honor before Allah, the Exalted. The description of angels as "noble" (الكرامة) is repeated in the Noble Quran. It is not unlikely—with the aid of the context—that what is meant is that by their very creation, they are protected from sin and disobedience, being fashioned upon impeccability (العصمة). This is supported by His saying: **بَلْ عِبادٌ مُكْرَمُونَ لا يَسْمِقُونَهُ بِالْقَوْلِ وَهُمْ بِأَمْرِهِ يَعْمَلُونَ** — *Rather they are [His] honored servants. They do not venture to speak ahead of Him, and they act by His command (21:26-27)*, as it indicates that they desire only what Allah desires and do only what He commands them. Likewise, His saying: **كِرامٍ بَرَرَةٍ** — *noble and pious (80:16)*.

What is meant by "writing" (الكتابة) in His saying **كاتِبِينَ** is the writing of deeds, by the evidence of His saying: **يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ** (who know whatever you do). A discussion regarding the meaning of the writing of deeds has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: **إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — *Indeed We used to record what you used to do (45:29)*; let whoever wishes refer to it.

His saying: **يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ** negates any error on their part in identifying good and evil or distinguishing between a righteous deed and a sin, just as the preceding verse implies their transcendence above sin and disobedience. Thus, they encompass the acts as they truly are in their attributes and preserve them as they truly are.

There is no specification in these verses regarding the number of these angels entrusted with writing the deeds of the human being. However, what is understood from His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ يَتَلَقَّى الْمُتَلَقِّيانِ عَنِ الْيَمِينِ وَعَنِ الشِّمالِ قَعِيدٌ** — *when the two receivers receive [him], seated on the right and on the left (50:17)*, is that over every human being are two of them, on his right and his left. It has been mentioned in the transmitted narrations (الروايات المأثورة) that the one on the right is the writer of good deeds and the one on the left is the writer of evil deeds.

It has also been mentioned in the commentary on His saying: **إِنَّ قُرْآنَ الْفَجْرِ كانَ مَشْهُوداً** — *Indeed the recital of dawn is witnessed (17:78)*, in widely transmitted reports (أخبار مستفيضة) from the paths of both schools, indicating that the writers of deeds by day ascend after sunset, and others descend to write the deeds of the night; then when dawn breaks, they ascend and the angels of the day descend, and so on.

In the verse—namely His saying: **يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ** — there is an indication that the writers are aware of the intentions (النيات), for there is no way to know the specificities of acts, their titles, and whether they are good or evil, righteous or sinful, except through knowledge of the intentions. Thus, their knowledge of the acts is not complete except through knowledge of the intentions.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ لَفِي نَعِيمٍ وَإِنَّ الْفُجَّارَ لَفِي جَحِيمٍ** — *Indeed the pious shall be in bliss, and indeed the vicious shall be in hell (82:13-14)*. This is a new sentence (استئناف) explaining the result of the preservation of deeds by the writing of the scribes and their manifestation on the Day of Resurrection.

The "pious" (الأبرار) are those whose deeds are good, and the "vicious" (الفجار) are those who breach boundaries with sins. It appears that what is meant by them are the brazen among the disbelievers, for no believer abides eternally in the Fire. The use of the indefinite (تنكير) for "bliss" (نعيم) and "hell" (جحيم) suggests magnification (التفخيم) and terror (التهويل)—as has been said.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَصْلَوْنَها يَوْمَ الدِّينِ** — *they shall enter it on the Day of Retribution (82:15)*. The pronoun refers to the blazing fire (الجحيم); i.e., they—the vicious—shall be bound to the Fire on the Day of Recompense and shall not part from it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما هُمْ عَنْها بِغائِبِينَ** — *and they shall not be absent from it (82:16)*. This is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسيري) to His saying: **يَصْلَوْنَها** etc., emphasizing the meaning of their being bound to the Fire and their eternal stay in it. What is meant by their "absence" from it is their exiting from it. Thus, the verse is in the sense of His saying: **وَما هُمْ بِخارِجِينَ مِنَ النَّارِ** — *and they shall not leave the Fire (2:167)*.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الدِّينِ** — *And what will show you what the Day of Retribution is? (82:17)*. This is to inspire awe (تهويل) and magnify (تفخيم) the matter of the Day of Judgment. The meaning is: you cannot encompass with knowledge the reality of the Day of Judgment. This expression is a metaphor for the magnitude of the matter and its loftiness beyond being reached by the description of any describer. The explicit mention of "the Day"—where a pronoun could have been used—is an emphasis on the magnification.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ ما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الدِّينِ** — *Again, what will show you what the Day of Retribution is? (82:18)*. The repetition of the sentence is an emphasis on the magnification.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ** — *It is a day when no soul will be of any avail to another soul, and all command that day will belong to Allah (82:19)*. The adverbial noun (الظرف) is in the accusative case by an implied "Remember" or similar. In the verse is a general exposition of the reality of the Day of Judgment after the urging to know it in His saying: **وَما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الدِّينِ**.

This is because the link of cause and effect (التأثير والتأثر) between apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) and their effects will be severed and vanished on that day, as understood from such verses as His saying, the Exalted: **وَتَقَطَّعَتْ بِهِمُ الْأَسْبابُ** — *and their means are cut off (2:166)*, and His saying: **وَلَوْ يَرَى الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا إِذْ يَرَوْنَ الْعَذابَ أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** — *That if the wrongdoers were to see—when they face the punishment—that power belongs entirely to Allah (2:165)*. Thus, no soul shall possess anything for another soul; it will not be able to avert an evil from it nor bring a good to it. This does not contradict the verses of intercession (الشفاعة), because that is by the permission of Allah; so He is the Owner (المالك) of it and none other.

His saying: **وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ** — *and all command that day will belong to Allah* — means He is the Owner of the command, and no one else has any part of the command.

What is meant by "command" (الأمر), as has been said, is the singular of "orders" (الأوامر), due to His saying, the Exalted: **لِمَنِ الْمُلْكُ الْيَوْمَ لِلَّهِ الْواحِدِ الْقَهَّارِ** — *‘To whom does the sovereignty belong today?’ ‘To Allah, the One, the All-paramount!’ (40:16)*. The status of the obeyed King is to command (الأمر) in the sense opposite to forbidding. "Command" in the sense of "affair" (الشأن) does not suit the context with such appropriateness.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذَا الْقُبُورُ بُعْثِرَتْ**, he said: "They split open and the people come out from them."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated from Hudhayfah, who said: The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: **من استن خيرا فاستن به فله أجره ـ ومثل أجور من اتبعه غير منتقص من أجورهم ـ ومن استن شرا فاستن به فله وزره ـ ومثل أوزار من اتبعه غير منتقص من أوزارهم** — "Whoever establishes a good tradition (سنة) which is then followed, he shall have its reward—and the like of the rewards of those who follow him without their rewards being diminished—and whoever establishes an evil tradition which is then followed, he shall bear its burden—and the like of the burdens of those who follow him without their burdens being diminished." Then Hudhayfah recited: **عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ وَأَخَّرَتْ**.

In the same source, ‘Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Salih ibn Mismar, who said: It reached me that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) recited this verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ ما غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ الْكَرِيمِ**, then he said: **جهله** — "His ignorance."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: **فِي أَيِّ صُورَةٍ ما شاءَ رَكَّبَكَ**, he said: "Had He willed, He would have composed you in a form other than this."

**I say:** It was also narrated in *al-Majma‘* from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه‌السلام) in a disconnected (مرسلا) report.

In the same source: **وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ**, he said: "The two angels entrusted with the human being."

From *Sa‘d al-Su‘ud*, and in a narration: "Indeed they—meaning the two entrusted angels—come to the believer at the time of the dawn prayer. When they descend, the two angels entrusted with the night ascend. When the sun sets, the two entrusted with writing the night descend to him, and the two angels who write by day ascend with his ledger (ديوانه) to Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.

This remains their habit until the time of his death arrives. When his death arrives, they say to the righteous man: 'May Allah reward you with good as a companion to us; how many a righteous deed you have shown us, how many a good word you have made us hear, and how many a good gathering you have brought us to. Today we are as you love, and intercessors to your Lord.' But if he was a sinner, they say to him: 'May Allah reward you with evil as a companion to us; you used to hurt us. How many an evil deed you have shown us, how many an evil word you have made us hear, and how many an evil gathering you have brought us to. Today we are against you as you dislike, and two witnesses before your Lord.'"

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ**, ‘Amr ibn Shimr narrated from Jabir from Abu Ja‘far (عليه‌السلام) that he said: **الأمر يومئذ واليوم كله لله. يا جابر إذا كان يوم القيامة بادت الحكام ـ فلم يبق حاكم إلا الله** — "The command on that day and every day belongs to Allah. O Jabir, when the Day of Resurrection comes, the rulers will perish, and no ruler will remain except Allah."

**I say:** His (عليه‌السلام) meaning is that the command belonging to Allah is not exclusive to the Day of Resurrection; rather, the command always belongs to Allah. Its specification to the Day of Resurrection is in terms of its manifestation (ظهوره), not its essence (أصله). What is exclusive to that Day is the manifestation of this reality through direct vision (ظهور عيان), so on that day, the command and judgment of anyone other than the Exalted falls away. Similar to "the command" are all other things counted in His speech as exclusive to the Day of Resurrection. Thus, the narration is among the most luminous of reports (غرر الروايات).
